query: clinical features of ethanol intoxication vs hypoglycemia

retrieved_docs.title: Alcohol Withdrawal Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: In the workup, it is important to consider clinical entities that exhibit signs and symptoms similar to alcohol withdrawal syndrome, such as thyrotoxicosis, hypoglycemia, diabetic ketoacidosis, stimulant substance use, status epilepticus, CNS infection, withdrawal from sedative-hypnotics, essential tremors, hepatic encephalopathy, intracranial hemorrhage, cerebrovascular accident, and psychosis. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthostatic Syncope (Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnosis includes other causes of loss of consciousness Seizures Hypoglycemia Concussion secondary to head trauma Drug or alcohol intoxication Metabolic conditions including hypothyroidism, hypoxemia

retrieved_docs.title: Isopropanol Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: After ingestion of isopropanol, patients typically present with altered mental status. Although the differential diagnosis of a patient with an altered mental status is extensive, the mnemonic “AEIOU-TIPS” should be employed to aid the physician in narrowing down the cause of a patient’s altered sensorium: A: Alcohol or acidosis E: Electrolytes or encephalopathy I: Infection O: Overdose or oxygen disorder U: Uremia T: Trauma or toxin I: Insulin P: Psychogenic S: Stroke or shock

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Glucose : Liver disease due to alcoholism may reduce glycogen stores, and ethanol impairs gluconeogenesis. As a consequence, patients with alcohol withdrawal develop anxiety, agitation, tremors, seizures, and diaphoresis, all of which can also occur with hypoglycemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Ethanol Toxicity -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Acute alcohol intoxication causes several metabolic abnormalities, including lactic acidosis, hypoglycemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia, and hypophosphatemia. Laboratory analysis should include a full electrolyte panel as well as liver function tests. Alcohol can cause acute effects on the cardiovascular system, such as atrial and ventricular tachydysrhythmias. An EKG should be obtained. One particular syndrome known as “holiday heart syndrome” can develop, which is characterized by new-onset arrhythmias following acute ingestion of alcohol and can include new-onset atrial fibrillation. Serial EKGs should be done if an arrhythmia is found, as the majority will resolve with the elimination of alcohol. If the EKG changes persist, an alternate cause should be considered. In the case of altered mental status, when a full history cannot be elucidated, a CT scan of the brain should be obtained to rule out any intracranial pathology contributing to the patient’s mental status. Many intoxicated patients may state suicidal thoughts or make such gestures. A psychiatric evaluation should be performed and may have to be repeated as the patient becomes more lucid. [1] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Diabetic Hypoglycemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol: It inhibits gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis. Patients intoxicated with alcohol have relatively low glycogen reserves, and alcohol also inhibits the shivering reflex, which leads to a poor endogenous response to hypoglycemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoglycemia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A detailed medication history History of alcohol or drug use History of psychiatric disorders Personal or family history of diabetes mellitus or multiple endocrine neoplasia syndromes (MEN) Unintentional weight changes Changes in medication Consideration of acute kidney injury or renal failure Symptoms of different hormone deficiencies. Timing of the hypoglycemic episode relative to meals or exercise.

retrieved_docs.title: Factitious Hypoglycemia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnosis of hypoglycemia includes but is not limited to the following: Alcohol intake Hormonal deficiency, e.g., cortisol Non-islet cell tumors Insulinoma Insulin autoimmune hypoglycemia Accidental or factitious hypoglycemia

retrieved_docs.title: Ethanol Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for acute ethanol toxicity is mostly supportive. The first priority, as always, is airway protection. The main life-threatening complication of alcohol intoxication is respiratory depression. Although most patients who present for alcohol intoxication receive intravenous fluids, there is no solid evidence to support this. Alcohol does act as a diuretic; thus, most patients who receive intravenous fluids are in an attempt to treat dehydration. As mentioned above, checking a point of care glucose is important, as many patients with alcohol use disorder will have depleted glycogen stores, and treating hypoglycemia is important, especially before replenishing vitamins such as thiamine. Few studies have shown vitamin deficiencies in intoxicated patients; thus, the routine use of IV multivitamins should be considered on a case-to-case basis. In contrast, routine use of thiamine is recommended for patients with alcohol use disorder, especially in the setting of altered mental status. Detecting occult thiamine deficiency and Wernicke encephalopathy is difficult, and this condition has a high mortality. Thus, the cost/benefit analysis falls in favor of administering thiamine. Patients with alcohol use disorder may not benefit from IV fluids, and consideration must be made for alcoholic cardiomyopathy in this patient population before administering fluids. Some patients may become agitated or violent. In these situations, sedative substances may be required, including droperidol or haloperidol, keeping in mind the potential interaction between the drug and alcohol. Depending on the severity of the intoxication and complications such as Wernicke encephalopathy, alcoholic hepatitis, or dysrhythmias, patients may have to be admitted to the hospital for further treatment. [1] [2] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Ethanol Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of ethanol toxicity. Outline the appropriate evaluation of ethanol toxicity. Review the management options available for ethanol toxicity. Describe interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication of ethanol toxicity and improving outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Ethanol Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for alcohol toxicity is very broad and includes anything that can cause an altered mental status. Considerations include trauma, sepsis, CNS infections, seizure, nonalcoholic toxicologic ingestion, hypo- or hyperthermia, hypo- or hyperthyroidism, hypoxia, and metabolic derangements. Many of these can coexist with alcohol toxicity, so it is important to have a low threshold to obtain laboratory workup and CNS imaging. Complicating the diagnosis of alcohol toxicity is the potential for the patient to ingest non-beverage alcohol such as cologne, cough syrup, and isopropyl alcohol. This can be accidental, such as in pediatrics, or intentional, such as in patients with alcohol use disorder who do not have access to alcohol. Ethylene glycol, methanol, and isopropyl toxicity are discussed in separate articles, and please see these articles for further details. Once alcohol use has been confirmed, further diagnoses have to be considered, such as Wernicke encephalopathy and hepatic encephalopathy. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis in HHS is divided into 2 broad groups: clinical conditions causing altered mental status and clinical conditions causing hyperglycemia. The following are some examples of conditions that can cause an alteration in mental status: Hypoglycemia Hyponatremia Hypernatremia Hyperammonemia Severe intoxication with drugs or alcohol Uremic encephalopathy Hyperglycemia can develop with diabetic ketoacidosis and diabetes insipidus. A detailed history, thorough physical examination, and use of ancillary studies can help to establish a diagnosis quickly. [23] HHS is a potentially fatal medical condition. Knowledge of pathophysiology and clinical presentation is necessary for the best management outcome.

retrieved_docs.title: Isopropanol Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Explore secondary causes of ketosis because ingestion of isopropanol causes ketosis without acidosis. Include diabetic ketoacidosis, alcoholic ketoacidosis, starvation ketosis, and other toxic alcohol ingestions in the differential.

retrieved_docs.title: Unconscious Patient -- Differential Diagnosis -- Systemic Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Unconsciousness can also result from various systemic causes, including hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia, hyponatremia, hypernatremia, hypercalcemia, seizures, systemic infections, meningitis, encephalitis, adrenal crisis, pituitary apoplexy with pituitary hormonal insufficiency, endocrine abnormalities, myxedema coma, medication overdose, illicit drug use, neuroleptic malignant syndrome, excessive alcohol intake, hepatic encephalopathy, uremia, heavy metal poisoning, malaria, aspergillosis, herbicides, carbon monoxide poisoning, and anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1514
retrieved_docs.content: Other endocrine disorders encompass نقص تركيز السكر (low blood sugar), indicating that hypoglycemia is classified within the broader group of endocrine abnormalities. Clinically, نقص تركيز السكر is associated with فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم (hyper‑kalemia), suggesting that patients with low glucose levels may also exhibit elevated serum potassium. Hyper‑kalemia itself shows clinical links to تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ (alcoholic intoxication), and it frequently co‑occurs with الحماض (acidosis), highlighting a common electrolyte and acid‑base disturbance in affected individuals. Alcoholic intoxication is further connected to الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية (abnormal chest X‑ray), reflecting possible pulmonary complications, and it gives rise to تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic intoxication), indicating liver involvement as a downstream effect. Together, these relationships illustrate a network in which endocrine dysfunction, electrolyte imbalance, acid‑base disorders, alcohol‑related toxicity, and organ‑specific imaging findings are interrelated, underscoring the importance of comprehensive assessment and coordinated management in patients presenting with any of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 325
retrieved_docs.content: “Disorder due to and following poisoning” is a broad diagnostic category that includes both the general condition “التسمم” (poisoning) and “poisoning by nonmedicinal substances.” Within this overarching group, “التسمم” is linked to several more specific clinical entities. It is associated with “تسمم بالأدوية المؤشرة على انحلال الليفين,” a form of drug‑induced poisoning characterized by leukocyte breakdown, and it is also associated with “متلازمة الأدرينالية” (adrenal syndrome), indicating that endocrine disturbances can accompany toxic exposures. Moreover, “التسمم الكحولي” (alcohol poisoning) is recognized as a particular subtype of the broader “التسمم” category, illustrating how alcohol‑related toxicity fits within the general framework of poisoning disorders. Together, these relationships demonstrate that poisoning disorders encompass a spectrum of etiologies—including non‑medicinal agents, specific drug toxicities, endocrine complications, and alcohol exposure—providing a comprehensive perspective for diagnosis, risk assessment, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: This hierarchical knowledge base connects each causative agent to its specific poisoning type, situates those poisonings within larger accidental or unspecified psychoactive poisoning frameworks, and delineates the spectrum of organic mental disorders and intoxication syndromes that may arise, thereby aiding clinicians in agent identification, poisoning classification, and anticipation of associated neuropsychiatric manifestations for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol intoxication (تَشَمُّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is clinically associated with hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم), indicating that patients who present with severe alcohol‑related toxicity are at risk for elevated serum potassium levels. In addition, hyperkalemia frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض), reflecting the common metabolic disturbance in which excess potassium and a lowered blood pH are observed together. Recognizing these interconnections is important for clinicians, as the presence of alcohol intoxication should prompt evaluation for hyperkalemia, and the detection of hyperkalemia should lead to assessment for accompanying acidosis to guide appropriate treatment and monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is clinically associated with several conditions, including hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر), alcoholic intoxication (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ), and abnormal chest radiography (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية). In turn, alcoholic intoxication is also clinically linked to hyperkalemia, reinforcing the bidirectional relationship between alcohol use and elevated serum potassium levels. Additionally, metabolic acidosis (الحماض) frequently co‑occurs with hyperkalemia, indicating that patients presenting with acidosis are likely to exhibit high potassium concentrations. The presence of an abnormal chest X‑ray may reflect underlying pulmonary or systemic effects of alcohol intoxication, which can contribute to the development of hyperkalemia. Together, these interconnections highlight the importance of evaluating blood glucose, alcohol exposure, acid‑base status, and chest imaging when assessing patients with elevated potassium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
retrieved_docs.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of disorders and clinical manifestations that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine system. It includes “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia), which represents elevated blood‑glucose levels and is a primary feature of diabetes. “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) is a severe, acute complication of uncontrolled hyperglycemia characterized by extreme dehydration and altered mental status. “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) denotes diabetes in its uncomplicated form, serving as the baseline condition from which other sequelae may develop. Among the chronic complications, “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy) reflects nerve damage caused by prolonged hyperglycemia, leading to sensory and motor deficits. “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy) is listed as a possible extra‑pancreatic manifestation that can be associated with underlying endocrine pancreatic disease. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), an excess of circulating insulin that may precede or accompany insulin resistance. Finally, “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) represents a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, producing a distinct clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse acute, chronic, and neoplastic conditions linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both glycemic control and associated complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1906
retrieved_docs.content: تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ, a specific form of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي, is clinically associated with فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم. This connection indicates that individuals with alcoholic encephalopathy may exhibit elevated blood potassium levels, underscoring the importance of monitoring electrolyte disturbances when managing hepatic encephalopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 323
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Disorder due to and following poisoning” serves as a broad classification that encompasses both general poisoning (التسمم) and poisoning by non‑medicinal substances. Within this framework, the specific condition of alcohol poisoning (التسمم الكحولي) is recognized as a distinct subtype of poisoning. The subcategory “Poisonings and Drug Effects” is also placed under the umbrella of poisoning, indicating that drug‑related toxic effects are considered part of the overall poisoning spectrum. Clinical observations link poisoning (التسمم) with adrenal syndrome (متلازمة الأدرينالية), highlighting an association that may be relevant for diagnosis and management of patients presenting with toxic exposures.

The endocrine pancreas disease spectrum (DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES) comprises hyperglycemia, elevated ketones, diabetic hyperosmolar coma, diabetes without complications, type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, hypoglycemia, disorders of gastrin secretion (اضطراب إفراز الجسترين), diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال عصبي سكري), progressive hemifacial atrophy (ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي), hyperinsulinemia (فرط أنسولين الدم) and glucagonoma (ورم غلوكاغوني). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1242
retrieved_docs.content: نقص تركيز السكر is associated with فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم (hyperkalemia), reflecting the electrolyte disturbances that can accompany severe low blood glucose. Hyperkalemia also co‑occurs with الحماض (acidosis), underscoring the metabolic derangements that frequently accompany each other in critical illness. Additionally, تشمّع كُحولِيّ (alcohol intoxication) is clinically linked to both فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم and abnormal chest radiographs (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية), suggesting that acute alcohol exposure may precipitate electrolyte imbalance and pulmonary findings. تشمّع كُحولِيّ further has a child condition, تشمُّع كَبِدي (liver intoxication), highlighting hepatic involvement in alcohol‑related toxicity. The disorder اضطراب إفراز الجسترين extends to a more detailed sub‑condition described as الحثل واضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ (intestinal mucosal atrophy and secretion disorders), indicating that abnormalities of gastric secretion can progress to

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: disorder (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات). Hyperglycemia/new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد) is placed within the dehydration group and includes polyuria (تأثير إدرار البول) as a characteristic feature. Overall, the knowledge base underscores the interdependence of lipid and carbohydrate metabolism, electrolyte and acid‑base homeostasis, endocrine‑pancreatic function, renal health, respiratory and hepatic pathology, and substance‑use effects, highlighting the necessity of integrated assessment and multidisciplinary management for patients presenting with these overlapping conditions.

This integrated hierarchy aids clinicians in understanding how different substance exposures and withdrawal states relate to one another and to shared toxicological categories, supporting comprehensive assessment and management of patients presenting with psychoactive‑substance intoxication or withdrawal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia is classified as a fluid and electrolyte disorder (حالات اضطراب توازن السائل والإلكتروليت والقاعدي الحمضي) and is linked to diabetic hyperosmolar coma (غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري), both of which fall under the broader category of acid‑base imbalance (اختلال التوازن الحمضي القاعدي). Acid‑base imbalance also comprises respiratory acidosis (تَقَلْوُن تنفسي). Acidosis (الحماض) is a specific type of fluid and electrolyte disorder and frequently co‑occurs with hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم). Hyperkalemia is clinically associated with hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر) and alcoholic intoxication (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ), the latter also being linked to abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية). Alcoholic hepatitis (الْتِهابُ الكَبِدِ الكحوليّ) is likewise associated with fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base disturbances, illustrating the interaction between liver disease and metabolic homeostasis. The category “Diseases of the Endocrine Pancreas Including Diabetes” is a subcategory of other endocrine disorders and includes hyperglycemia (ارتفاع السكر في الدم), diabetic hyperosmolar coma, hypoglycemia, gastrin secretion disorder (اضطراب إفراز الجسترين), diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال عصبي سكري), hyperinsulinemia (فرط أنسولين الدم), and glucagonoma (ورم غلوكاغوني). Hypoglycemia is also listed directly under other endocrine disorders. Gastrin secretion disorder further leads to gastric mucosal atrophy and secretion disorders (الحثل واضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ). These interrelated entities demonstrate how disturbances in fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance intersect with endocrine pancreatic diseases, hepatic pathology, and alcohol‑related conditions, underscoring the need for integrated assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: toxic alcohol exposure can intersect with fluid‑electrolyte disturbances. Overall, the interconnections among diabetes, hyperosmolar states, renal failure, acid‑base and electrolyte abnormalities, gastrointestinal secretion disorders, and alcohol‑related toxicity underscore the need for comprehensive, integrated assessment and management in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is clinically associated with low blood glucose (نقص تركيز السكر), and it frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض). This interrelationship indicates that patients who present with hyperkalemia should be evaluated for possible hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis, as the simultaneous presence of these disturbances can affect diagnosis, treatment decisions, and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1922
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic hepatitis (الْتِهابُ الكَبِدِ الكحوليّ) is clinically associated with disorders of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance (حالات اضطراب توازن السائل والإلكتروليت والقاعدي الحمضي). Acidosis (الحماض) is itself classified as a disorder of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance. Consequently, both alcoholic hepatitis and acidosis involve or are linked to disturbances in fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base homeostasis, underscoring the need for careful monitoring and management of these metabolic derangements in affected patients.
